data.title: Meta Q1 Earnings Preview: AI Expansion And Its Ad Business, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) ended its fiscal 2025 with record quarterly sales and a forecast for a massive increase in CAPEX through 2026. I’ll be watching whether META can keep the good times rolling into its first fiscal quarter of 2026, set for release on Wednesday, the 29th.

Shares currently are up about 13% over the past month, but are still essentially flat on a YTD basis.

While the stock trades at the upper boundaries of its 52-week range, its not overly expensive, in my view. META trades at a 22x forward, and that is in-line with its own historicals as well as to the broader sector.

The stock doesn’t grade as bullishly by Seeking Alpha’s (“SA”) quants, and that is due to META’s existing valuation metrics. For my part, I don’t necessarily agree with the rating here. META is reporting over 20% YOY sales growth and is operating on 40% margins. I believe the current multiples are a fair price to pay for that growth.

At the other end of the spectrum are ratings from the Wall Street community, the consensus of which is a Strong Buy with an attached price target of $855/share.

That would imply nearly 30% upside potential from current levels. I’m not a chaser of momentum, and while I view this as attainable, I would need more insight from the upcoming earnings release.

Ahead of the Q1 release, I view META as reasonably priced and see shares as a Hold.

What Is The Outlook For META Stock?

As it left its fiscal 2025, META looked to me like an emerging AI infrastructure engine that happens to own the world’s largest attention platforms. I believe this emergence is going to continue through 2026.

In his commentary, CEO Mark Zuckerberg made it clear that 2026 would shape up to be the year where META’s AI investments start showing up in a much more visible way across products, ads, and even how the company operates internally.

With more than 3.5B people using at least one of its apps daily, I believe that META has an unmatched data and engagement loop that it is now pairing with next-generation recommendation systems and LLM integration.

In my view, that’s already translating into big time gains. Q4 ad impressions, for example, rose 18%, pricing improved 6%, and AI-driven ranking improvements translated to increases in views, clicks, and conversions across Facebook and Instagram.

Of special note is that this is happening without materially increasing ad load. Instead, META is getting far better at placing the right ad at the right moment. To me, this is a much more durable lever for revenue growth.

At the same time, META appears to be leaning hard into infrastructure, forecasting massive CAPEX through 2026.

One concern here is the ancillary expense that could result. While expenses are rising, the company still posted a 41% operating margin in Q4, and I expect that margin prowess to continue through 2026.

How May Recent Headlines Figure Into Q1?

Recent headlines around META all seem to point in the same direction. That is, its AI investments are directly translating into ad dominance, product momentum, and infrastructure commitments that support the long-term bull thesis.

Here’s one example. In a recent news clipper, it was reported that eMarketer expects META to surpass Google parent, Alphabet (GOOG), this year in net digital ad revenue. This is both a symbolic and financial milestone.

Furthermore, that’s being driven largely by AI-powered engagement on Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp, where META patiently built user behavior before introducing ads. Reels, alone, is reportedly on pace for a +$50B revenue run rate, aided by recommendation improvements that lifted U.S. watch time more than 30%.

On the AI front, the release of Muse Spark signals to me that META is re-entering the frontier model race in a serious way, while the multi-year custom silicon partnership with Broadcom (AVGO) shows just how committed the company is to owning its infrastructure stack through MTIA chips and data center expansion.

At the same time, planned layoffs of roughly 10% of staff echo the earlier “year of efficiency,” suggesting META is reshaping its workforce around AI productivity gains.

What To Watch When META Reports Q1?

When META reports Q1, aside from the headline figures, I’ll be tuned in to the commentary around AI progress, monetization efficiency, and how the company is managing an unprecedented step-up in spending.

First, I’ll be watching how results track relative to the prior Q1 revenue guide of +$53.5B–$56.5B and whether META tightens or lifts its full-year outlook. With eMarketer projecting META to overtake GOOG in net ad revenue this year, any evidence that Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp ads are accelerating faster than expected would be worthy of broader attention.

Second, updates on AI integration into the ad stack will be key. In Q4, model improvements drove increases in clicks, conversions, and ad quality without increasing ad load. If that trend continues, it likely would support the idea that META’s growth is coming from better monetization, not just more ads.

Third, I’ll be listening for updates on infrastructure and custom silicon progress following the partnership with AVGO. CAPEX is set to surge through 2026, so investors will definitely want some reassurance that these investments are translating into performance gains.

Finally, commentary around planned layoffs and internal AI productivity gains could signal META's commitment about becoming a leaner, AI-native organization.

Is META Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

I continue to view META as one of the best names in large cap tech today. That said, I am not necessarily one to chase at current levels.

In my view, META appears to definitely be evolving into an AI-driven platform with unmatched scale, improving efficiency in how it serves ads, and a very deliberate plan to own the infrastructure needed to power the next wave of products.

The company is showing that AI is not only improving engagement across Facebook, Instagram, Reels, Threads, and WhatsApp, but also materially improving advertiser performance without leaning harder on ad load. I believe that’s a powerful and durable growth lever.

At the same time, the company is embarking on one of the largest capital investment cycles in its history, with CAPEX set to surge through 2026. While margins remain very strong today, I would like more clarity on how these investments translate into tangible returns over the next 12–24 months.

I ultimately believe that shares trade at a fair multiple for a company growing revenue over 20% with 40% operating margins. I also believe the long-term story is very compelling. However, with the stock near its highs, I view META as a Hold and would prefer to add on a more meaningful pullback and after gaining additional insight from this earnings report.

data.title: Oracle Is The Winner From The OpenAI vs. Anthropic War
data.description:
Oracle (ORCL) has officially become a forgotten AI winner. The initial reason was apparently due to the heavy customer concentration to OpenAI, but the main driver over the last few months has been the ongoing suffering in the software sector. Investors may be overlooking the positive developments taking place at OpenAI and the AI market overall, as it is looking increasingly likely that ORCL will be able to capture the upside from its ginormous cloud backlog. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

ORCL Stock Price

I last covered ORCL in December, where I stuck by bullishness amidst the apocalypse in SaaS stocks. The stock has flatlined since.

The market appears to be underestimating the resilience of the software business while somehow ignoring the growing momentum in the cloud infrastructure business.

ORCL Stock Key Metrics

ORCL was once known as a legacy software company, and that title likely explains the stock’s struggles in recent quarters. The company’s software offerings, including those in ERP and HCM, have historically proven to be very sticky and reliable businesses.

The company’s financial performance, however, shows a different focus. Cloud revenues jumped 44% YoY in the most recent quarter to $8.9 billion, crossing the crucial 50% threshold of revenues. Sure, software revenues grew by only 3% YoY (and actually declined on a constant currency basis), but slow software growth is nothing new for the company. I expect cloud infrastructure revenues to soon take over as the most important business line for the company.

The company has seen cloud revenue growth accelerate for four straight quarters as it has proven to be a true winner of generative AI.

The cloud growth is being driven by growth in cloud infrastructure revenues, which jumped 84% YoY to $4.9 billion.

The company saw remaining performance obligations (‘RPOs’) increase $29 billion sequentially to $553 billion, representing 325% YoY growth. Crucially, the company noted that most of the sequential growth comes from deals in which the company will not need to raise additional funding to execute the contracts. This is an important detail because the company maintains a highly leveraged balance sheet. The company ended the quarter with $39 billion in cash and $2.2 billion in non-marketable securities (primarily consisting of its 15% equity stake in TikTok) versus $135 billion in debt. The company’s net debt position is a stark contrast to the net cash positions at the competing hyperscalers. That, however, has not prevented the company from winning cloud compute deals with OpenAI and others. The AI story has been so strong that we have seen exponential growth take place across the tech stack, ranging from previously niche players like Lumentum (LITE) and Coherent (COHR) down to Micron (MU). I view ORCL as being in the same vein, but it has somehow not benefitted from a similar re-rating as the hardware suppliers. Over time, I expect investors to warm up again to the hyperscalers, as cloud computing is arguably a key “picks and shovels” play for AI.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to deliver around 20% revenue growth in the fourth quarter, in line with this most recent quarter. Management also increased their revenue guidance for the upcoming fiscal year to $90 billion, representing 34% YoY growth and up from the prior guide of $85 billion.

On the conference call, management pushed back on the AI disruption threat, noting that they “would be a threat if we weren't adopting them, but we are and very rapidly.” I think that this is an important detail that the market is overlooking. Sure, AI might lead to pressure to traditional software business models, but this overlooks two details. First, ORCL is aggressively integrating AI solutions into their existing software products, but more importantly, software companies like ORCL in theory should be the greatest beneficiaries of any AI-led cost optimizations. Case in point: ORCL has reportedly already begun a significant round of layoffs. Management notes that their software solutions are mission-critical and are likely to be among the lowest priority for customers to replace with competing AI solutions. Management notes that “data gravity matters” which may also be relevant given ORCL’s rising push into cloud computing.

Is ORCL Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

One can be forgiven for forgetting that ORCL once was viewed as a hot stock and growing hyperscaler. Or was it just me? Investors appear to be discounting the company’s torrid growth rates and focused on the potential for software struggles. This skepticism looks misplaced, especially as OpenAI looks poised to take advantage of their computing capacity in their competition versus Anthropic. The company is expected to post stunning growth over the coming years, mainly driven by providing cloud capacity to OpenAI.

That is expected to lead to tremendous earnings growth, with the stock trading at around 9x 2030e earnings.

It is worth noting that consensus estimates are more or less in line with management’s targets given at its 2025 Analyst Day meeting.

I’d note that while one always needs to be cautious on growth estimates, in this case there is great visibility in the growth trajectory due to the large backlog, and consensus estimates are implying a mere 26% net margin in FY2030, which looks quite achievable given that ORCL is likely to be a beneficiary of AI efficiencies. There are two main questions to answer here: How resilient is the software business, and is the OpenAI business high quality? I view ORCL’s software as being the kind that customers aren’t eager to vibe code themselves or move to cheaper AI-built competitors. These are the kinds of software where data integrity and reputational risk loom large, making the risk of switching away greater than the potential cost savings. The real potential risk to the software is instead that of seat displacements as companies find more and more ways to use AI to replace human labor. Yet in this scenario, I would expect ORCL to always be able to reduce its own expenses more, given that software development (including sales, support, and others) would appear to be the most AI-applicable targets for efficiency. Moving on to the OpenAI cloud business. Yes, customer concentration of this magnitude should always be approached with caution, especially if OpenAI ends up losing out to other models (or hyperscalers). That said, OpenAI currently appears to be on strong footing. The company clearly benefits from a first-mover advantage, which I expect to persist. Each new release from the AI models feels more cutting-edge than the previous. I expect these improvements to eventually lead to a “tipping point” in which both enterprise and consumer adoption of AI accelerate. Whereas only a few months ago one might have been skeptical of why OpenAI needed to so aggressively sign so many large contracts, that aggression is looking more and more justified in hindsight. It looks like the AI labs are in a race to win market share, and ORCL stands to directly benefit as it delivers on its computing contracts. I expect ORCL to command at least a 20x earnings multiple in 2030, corresponding to a conservative 1x price-to-earnings-growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). That low 1x PEG ratio would allow the high growth to be offset by the concentration and software uncertainty risk. At current prices, the stock looks priced for around 23% compounded annual returns over the next four years. I note that it is important to factor in any potential returns from GAAP earnings due to the likelihood that the company will need to reinvest heavily in building out its data center footprint.

ORCL Stock Risks

It is possible that the software business really is outright disrupted by AI. Perhaps ORCL is unable to offset the disruption through cost optimizations of its own. It is possible that ORCL’s cloud growth is merely low-quality revenue, with low margins and low switching costs. The AI story might be paused due to macro factors such as rising inflation or difficulties in building out data centers. It is possible that the company is unable to fund its cloud growth due to its leveraged balance sheet. Interest rates might rise, leading to pressure to the bottom line due to rising interest expenses.

ORCL Stock Conclusion

It is unclear when the clouds over software will pass, if ever. I believe that ORCL’s software offerings look mission-critical and more resilient than most, and that the company is likely to be more of a beneficiary from AI-optimizations than a victim. It is, however, the company’s fast-growing cloud business that deserves the bulk of the attention. With OpenAI looking poised to engage in aggressive competition with Anthropic, I expect ORCL to more or less fully deliver on its cloud backlog. ORCL is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: Applied Optoelectronics Has A Vision Of Success, And So Do I
data.description:
There’s a reason Applied Optoelectronics, Inc. (AAOI) has attracted so much attention lately. It’s no longer the small optical supplier it used to be, but it’s also not merely a “hot sector” move.

It’s a stock that’s becoming a vertically integrated hyperscaler with an AI-optics story that’s only getting stronger. The company has spent years building in-house optical technology, and now that the market needs a lot more high-speed optical connectivity for AI, things are really taking off!

Applied Optoelectronics may have already had a massive run, but it certainly doesn’t look like they're done. After looking at the company’s latest report, their recent customer activity, and their improving margins, I would say this is still a compelling buying opportunity.

This isn’t a company that’s merely clinging to hope to survive, it’s one that’s finally able to turn its vertical integration, connections, and product roadmap into meaningful numbers.

For a long time, the market hasn’t given Applied Optoelectronics much credit. It was seen as a volatile optical-component supplier. Its history is marked by huge swings, heavy customer concentration, and painful setbacks when its larger customers decided to pull back.

So while the market’s skepticism made sense at the time, it’s also what makes the story that much more interesting now that the company appears to have broken away from that period and into a much more favorable demand cycle. They now have a stronger product mix and a more strategic role than they did before in hyperscaler infrastructure.

Applied Optoelectronics has long emphasized that a large portion of its data-center transceivers use their own in-house lasers and light engines, which they say give them a competitive advantage. They aren’t just putting together optical products using commodity third-party parts anymore. Their in-house design and manufacturing capabilities allows them to control more of the stack.

And in a market where speed, performance, availability, and cost are all such critical components, we all know that vertical integration can help improve both responsiveness and margins.

So now that AI infrastructure is creating a massive need for faster optical interconnects, especially in hyperscaler datacenters, Applied Optoelectronics is really showing the market its worth.

They are currently positioning themselves around 800G and 1.6T transceivers, which is one of the biggest reasons the stock has risen as dramatically as it has. Last month, the company announced a new order for 800G data-center transceivers from a major hyperscaler.

But that’s not all, Barron reported that the company had also received a $71 million order for their transceivers, bringing the total orders to $124 million since mid-March.

Now THAT’s a good sign.

Applied Optoelectronics isn’t just a theoretical AI beneficiary, it’s a company getting real orders tied to real infrastructure demand.

But let’s take a look at the numbers.

Applied Optoelectronics ten-year average total return is 23.82% (vs. the S&P 500’s 23.4%), their five-year average total return is 73.78% (vs. the S&P 500’s 13.4%), their three-year average total return is 296.34% (vs. the S&P 500’s 22.1%), their twelve-month total return is 1145.7% (vs. the S&P 500’s 37.0%), their three-month total return is 303.8% (vs. the S&P 500’s -1.4%) and their one-month total return is 39.5% (vs. the S&P 500’s 1.3%).

If that isn’t some of the most impressive alpha I’ve seen in a while, I don’t know what is!

However, those aren’t the only numbers we need to look at. In Q4 2025, Applied Optoelectronics reported revenue of $134.3 million, which was up from $100.3 million in the same quarter a year earlier and up from $118.6 million in Q3 2025.

Their GAAP gross margin improved to 31.2%, versus 28.7% in the prior-year period and 28.0% in the previous quarter. And their full-year 2025 revenue rose to $476.0 million from $260.4 million in 2024, while full-year GAAP gross margin improved to 28.6% from 22.6%.

However, the company still posted a GAAP net loss for the full year, but that loss narrowed materially to $13.6 million from $65.2 million in 2024.

So while the numbers had certainly gotten better, by the end of that report, we can’t say they're "good" per se. Just that they weren’t as bad as they were before. But what those numbers are a sign of is that the company is heading in the right direction!

Afterall, Applied Optoelectronics isn’t a pristine, fully mature compounder. It’s a business that’s finally showing the kind of operational improvement and demand alignment that can support a much higher-quality earnings profile than the market used to assign to it.

Barron’s reported that Microsoft had become the company’s largest customer, while Amazon had made a performance-based investment that could total $4 billion over time. Investor’s Business Daily similarly reported a 10-year, $4 billion purchase agreement tied to Amazon.

These deals don’t mean there isn’t any risk, what they mean is that Applied Optoelectronics is operating in the major leagues now, which is a lot different than the little leagues it was in during many of its more difficult years. It also says a lot about them as a company. Afterall, a company doesn’t just attract that kind of strategic commercial attention by accident.

What got them to where they are today is their foresight and execution.

They didn’t just all of a sudden decide to care about optical performance and vertical integration. No, they’ve already been building around those advantages for years.

So what’s changed?

Well, with the rise of AI clusters, hyperscaler infrastructure demand and high-speed networking demand, the market has finally moved in a direction that makes Applied Optoelectronics capabilities stand out.

Their ability to manufacture in-house matters much more now than it ever did before, and that isn’t because of luck, it's because their long-term strategy has finally paid off. That’s some serious foresight.

And that strategic foresight is even showing up in their production. Investor’s Business Daily reported that the company had been ramping 800G transceiver production in the U.S. and Taiwan and have a target of more than 100,000 units per month!

That matters a lot more than some people think, because where many smaller hardware names stumble is being able to produce enough to meet their demand. If Applied Optoelectronics can execute that ramp effectively, I’d say the company has a much stronger case for sustained operating leverage!

This is the time where valuations become extremely important.

In my valuation, I am using the next-year EPS estimate of $4.64, as I assume Applied Optoelectronics will take advantage of their new position to become much more profitable quickly. As their recent financial improvement looks to be only the beginning of a MUCH larger operating-leverage phase.

I assume that their foresight thus far will continue as their 800G shipments continue to ramp up and their product mix continues to shift toward higher-value datacenter optics. The forward P/E ratio is set to 28.73X, and the five-year annual growth rate is set to 15%.

Applied Optoelectronics has shown real growth, and AI-driven optical demand has only continued to rise. Not only that, but the company has already described a $150 million expansion in Sugar Land, Texas, to focus on AI datacenter transceivers.

The PEG ratio is 1.92X, and the five-year target price is calculated to be $244.84. I assume that Applied Optoelectronics’ shift from niche optical vendor to scaled AI interconnect supplier is based on their foresight and execution instead of luck, and that’s something that only gets rewarded more as time goes on.

IBD reports that annual revenue could approach $1.91 billion by 2027, with $1.52 billion coming from data centers alone. All of this results in a five-year return potential of 83.69%. Expectations for Applied Optoelectronics are no longer low. It has become a momentum-backed optical and AI infrastructure name with a lot of hope and a lot of demand.

However, that means the risk is more real than ever.

With the main risk still being customer concentration, the same thing that hindered much of the stock’s growth in the past. The company even acknowledged that it remains dependent on a relatively small number of customers for a fairly substantial portion of its revenue.

Which means that changes in the size or timing of those customer's orders can affect the revenue big time. So, investors need to pay strong attention to any major hyperscalers that are working with Applied Optoelectronics. If those customers decide to slow orders, delay their rollouts, or change suppliers, the stock will likely react hard.

Another risk is that profitability isn't normalized yet. While Applied Optoelectronics did improve meaningfully in 2025, its full-year GAAP was still a net loss. So while the direction is clearly better, the company hasn’t fully completed the turnaround, at least not from a bottom-line perspective.

The market is still pricing in future profitability as much as current profitability, that’s not stable. The stock could very quickly re-rate lower if the margin gains stall or revenue ramp ends up disappointing.

Of course, competition is also a very real risk. Applied Optoelectronics isn’t the biggest name in the industry, and the optical and networking space definitely has larger players with broader footprints than they do.

But while Applied Optoelectronics’ edge is that it has a vertically integrated model and appears to be getting traction in a very attractive piece of the market, that isn’t enough. Execution HAS to stay sharp for them to stand out.

But one of the key risks is that Applied Optoelectronics has just had a huge run. A stock that has already moved as much as they have can only keep going up if fundamentals continue to improve, and while I see the foundation of foresight and execution, I know that the market will be much less forgiving if anything slips.

However, Applied Optoelectronics is absolutely the kind of name that can gap up hard on an order announcement, so investors really just need to pay attention. Luckily for my customers, my eyes are always watching. Applied Optoelectronics is in my Emerging Growth portfolio, and that’s one I watch very, very closely.

Here’s the thing, when I take a good step back and look at everything, what I see is a company with a much better setup than it had for most of the last decade. I don’t see a company that got lucky or is falling into old habits.

Revenue is growing, the margins are improving, losses are narrowing, and high-speed optical demand is accelerating! And while major hyperscalers are obviously a big part of the story, what really stands out to me is Applied Optoelectronics’ foresight.

Its in-house optical technology is clearly what the market is looking for, and as AI isn’t showing any signs of slowing down, I would be a fool to think that Applied Optoelectronics is either. That’s why I remain bullish, and why I see Applied Optoelectronics as a Buy.

data.title: Brookfield Renewable: Stock Gets Expensive, 7.75% Preferreds Still Make Sense
data.description:
We have written about Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) and its corporate counterpart, Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC), quite a few times. While we have had buy ratings on these at times, we generally have been fairly conscious of the valuation. Since our last article, BEP has taken off. We go over the recent results and news stories and tell you why valuation is pushing into the stratosphere. While the BEP units make no sense whatsoever, we do give some plays that offer a better bang for your buck.

Earnings and the Big Rally

BEP's numbers are measured using funds from operations (FFO), and the stock does not appear particularly expensive using those.

15X is a discount if you tag it to the market P/E multiple. The latter is hovering near 22X. BEP also offers some decent growth prospects, and in their case, we would say it is more of a "lock" than anything in the broader market.

While those may be reasons to like the company, we remain skeptical. The reason is that the valuation is not just the FFO. BEP carries a massive, massive debt load, and its net debt to EBITDA is 6.0X. While 6.0X may sound acceptable within the realm of quality triple net assets from REITs, BEP has about 60% of its EBITDA coming from assets with a limited lifespan (solar, wind). Sure, the hydro segment has an extremely long (unlimited for our calculations), but the fact that the company is trading at 15X EV to EBITDA is a serious negative for a long-side case for us.

Getting back to this fantastic rally, some might extrapolate this coming from higher earnings estimates. As we have mentioned in several articles, a change in earnings (FFO here) estimates is usually a driver of alpha. BEP unfortunately has not seen those changes and has rallied nonetheless.

What is underpinning this sector's strength is the paradoxical data center FOMO. The word on the street is that there isn't enough power to meet the data center demand, and this is causing a chase for all power producers. We think it is backward-looking, as the surge in demand is already fixing itself via cancellations and delays of new projects.

Between a third and a half of all US data centers planned for 2026 are likely to be delayed or totally cancelled, Bloomberg reports, amid ongoing supply chain challenges and campus location concerns.

With an estimated 12-16GW of planned capacity, only 5GW is currently under construction and many projects remain having been announced, but with no physical progress yet.

Maybe one of the most notable is OpenAI's $500 billion Stargate Project, with progress reportedly having stalled.

Source Tech Radar

Of course the market will ignore facts as long as it wants to ignore facts. But the most we would pay for BEP (or BEPC for that matter) would be $25.

Preferreds/Subordinated Debt

BEP has three securities that function as preferred shares. We say "function" as three out of four are actually called junior subordinated debt. But all hold the same credit rating, and in a distressed situation, they will be hit equally. These are,

1) Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. 5.25% PFD CL A (BEP.PR.A)

2) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. 4.625% SUB NTS (BEPH)

3) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. PERPETUAL SUB NTS (BEPI)

4) Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc. 7.250% SUB NOTES (BEPJ)

All four carry BBB- ratings.

We will point out that the company has a BBB+ rating, while the preferred securities are rated two notches lower at BBB-.

Fitch Ratings has affirmed Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. 's Long-Term Issuer Default Ratingat 'BBB+' and Short-Term IDR at 'F2'. Fitch has also affirmed Brookfield Renewable Partners ULC's unsecured notes at 'BBB+' and fixed-to-fixed subordinated notes at 'BBB-' and the preferred instruments issued by BEP and Brookfield Renewable Power Preferred Equity Inc. at 'BBB-'. Fitch has also affirmed Brookfield BRP Holdings (Canada) Inc.'s perpetual subordinated notes at 'BBB-' and its CP program at 'F2'. The Rating Outlook is Stable.

BEP's ratings and Outlook are supported by its large and diversified portfolio of contracted hydro, solar, wind and storage assets, as well as a supportive political and economic environment for renewable generation. The ratings and Outlook reflect Fitch's assumption that growth will be executed in a credit-supportive manner and Holdco-only funds from operations (FFO) leverage will stay below 4x on an annualized run-rate basis.

Source: Fitch

What is notable here is that the four offer strong yields from investment-grade instruments. BEP.PR.A offers 7.25% stripped yield as we write this, while the other three are actually closer to 7.75%. If you look at securities in general on the last rung of investment grade, you tend to get 6.0% to 6.5% on those. AT&T preferreds which include AT&T Inc. 5% DEP RP PFD A (T.PR.A) currently yield exactly in the middle of this range. So BEP's 7.75% yields are rather strong for their credit rating. Some investors might be concerned about the 6X debt to EBITDA we previously mentioned, and that is definitely something that needs to be looked into. But the BEP balance sheet is definitely very protective for the preferreds as 90% of the debt is non-recourse.

So if something goes wrong on a project, BEP walks away and leaves the debt holders to figure out how to extract value from that renewable energy project. We think the BEP preferred yields at 7.75% probably represent one of the highest risk-adjusted yields you can find. They are also well below par, and that creates no real risk of a call. Of course, should a call occur, it will be extremely profitable.

Verdict

The chase for BEP shows FOMO. But that does not even compare to the FOMO for BEPC. In order to avoid the "dreaded K-1," investors were recently ponying up $13 extra per share.

We also think this is a good chart for sentiment. You generally want to buy BEP (or BEPC) when the spread is extremely low (less than $2.00). That tells us that the entry point for these two is far ahead in the future and not now. Currently the secondary securities make the most sense.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Lucky Strike: Confirmation Of The Volatility And Downside
data.description:
Sometimes, companies that I invest in that are directly or indirectly linked to other companies I invest in can present an opportunity for investment. For Bowlero, now known as Lucky Strike Entertainment Corporation (LUCK), I consider this, unfortunately, to not be the case. In fact, I would say that LUCK, as well as the company I considered to be attractive before it became involved with it (which would be VICI), have become markedly less attractive over time, though the reasons for the two doing so are somewhat different.

LUCK has some advantages and some definite positives at a high-level review and overview. We're looking at a market leader in bowling entertainment, great diversification on a geographical basis, a good asset portfolio, a good track record of accretive M&As, and decent FCF and overall cash flow generation.

I'd also go ahead and call the management team for the company to be both capable and proven. We can confirm the company's capabilities by looking at its rate of top-line growth, which is, in fact, excellent if looked at on a high level and longer-term.

This revenue increase is also visible on a per-location basis. A positive also is some of the company's other financial developments, such as CapEx reductions, cash flow increases, and the like. As I mentioned in my last article, the company has also been expanding its operations to things like water parks. The company is a play on experiential assets, with a current focus on parks and bowling.

It's fair to say, however, that the company is not exactly convincing its skeptics right now (of which I am one). Current estimate averages and valuation averages come to about "HOLD", with both SA analysts and Quant at those "HOLD" ratings. The problem, at least if you look at it from a high level, seems to be profitability for the company, and the dividend at only 2.67% is not exactly considered safe here.

In my last article, I went through some of the company's issues - here I mean to update on them.

Lucky Strike Corporation - Problems Seem To Be There, And Profitability Is So-So

Reviewing the company's numbers, I am reminded that there are some fundamental issues in the company's profitability, but I am also reminded by forecasts that these issues may be on the way to being solved in the coming years.

Current estimates for AFFO include a significant increase in 2026E and another in 2028E. If the current analyst targets materialize, we'd be talking about FCFE of about $1.7/share in 2028E, which would be more than 5x the FCFE for the period of 2025 and would obviously warrant a higher valuation.

So why do I believe this development and this target are unlikely to materialize?

I've spoken previously about the significant leverage. The current estimates, which are also reflected by a very high rate of uncertainty in the forecast accuracy, assume growth in the company's FCFE, but in my view, without making proper accounting for the impact of about 6-8x leverage. The company's financial structure is somewhat sensitive. The company currently generates about $1.2B worth of revenue per year, but the company's operating margins have unfortunately been declining since 2022 and currently stand at less than 12%. Because of year-over-year rising interest costs, the company cannot be considered all that safe, because these interest costs lead to negative net income.

While net income cannot be said to be the most significant metric or KPI for a REIT, it's still something I'd want to look at when evaluating a company. The fact is that the diluted EPS has been negative for all but 2023 for the past 5 years, which means that the company's dividend cannot be considered sustainable. Net debt has been rising significantly - in less than 3 years, it's pretty much tripled. If the company had seen significant improvement from this or a time for profitability, I would be able to excuse this, but so far, this has not been the case. The company's liabilities in fact outnumber its assets, which in layman's terms means that the company has a negative net worth, or what in some circles is known as "balance sheet insolvency". In essence, selling all assets at value would likely not even cover Lucky Strike's outstanding debt. The wording is harsh, and it's obviously important to understand that this does not equate to actual financial risk of bankruptcy, but it's worth noting.

The company's strategy has seen it shift to a sort of "premium" bowling and waterparks play, as mentioned above. But this is not a net positive, even if done with less debt. The introduction of water parks introduces volatility to something that was previously relatively stable. Then there's the obvious pressure of inflation, labor costs, and the like, all of which seem designed to bring this company down further.

For this reason, I require the company to provide me with actionable and concrete data as to why I should consider it more attractive than before. You could argue that the company has a positive FCF of about 3%, but I would then argue the company spends almost as much capital on SBC as it generates in FCF, depending on the year - so this is hardly a positive.

All of the above, including the negative net worth, means that the company's book value, or NAV, is actually negative. Recent results, published about 2 months ago, offer little positivity for the near term. The company's revenues are up about 0.3% on a same-store revenue basis, but LUCK also reported a net loss of $12.7M for 2Q26, with a further decline in EBITDA (about $20M). Management argues it to be an inflection point to deliver same-center sales growth - I disagree with this assessment, given that the company has yet to show any sort of bottom-line improvement, and this set of results does not help. Revenue is expected to rise to about $1.3B. However, because the EBITDA is adjusted, if you were to look at it on an unadjusted basis, it turns negative - thus, I don't consider this to be good enough yet. Despite revenue growth, the company has repeatedly missed earnings expectations, and losses are continuing well into the new fiscal year. Management has already said that not all SG&A/marketing spend will drive ROI due to things like labor costs.

My question is simple: when can a conservative investor expect profitability out of LUCK?

Currently, the company expects to turn a new leaf with profitability next quarter and in the fourth quarter of 2026. That means that we'll want to keep an eye on earnings in May, because this is when the next quarter's results are coming.

I, however, remain dubious about this target.

Valuation Remains High For The 3Q26 Period, And I Do Not View Profitability As Likely

The reason that I view profitability as likely in the 3Q26 period is based on the results for the previous period, as well as the underlying pressures on the company's bottom line. There's still substantial debt, there's operational pressure, and there's a relatively negative trend ongoing. From what I can read, the company's recent pressure is not related to any one-time effect or non-recurring negative; the decline in net income, therefore, seems to me like it's one that's likely to remain.

The company's declining trend of operating margin also suggests that this is the case. The decline from over 15.5% to less than 11% is a drop of more than 400 bps, which is significant, and while total revenue has grown above 2%, the same-store sales imply with flat growth that there's underlying trouble in the company's locations.

The issue with this sort of debt is that it's a hole that, for the company, is very tricky to dig itself out of. It's clear from recent trends that the macroeconomic pressures across the US are also contributing to the declines in the company's results. A double-digit decline in business events has impacted the company's results, with implications that customers are trying to save money.

I consider it far more likely, as the results and forecasts look now, that Lucky Strike will remain unprofitable throughout 2026. I further consider it unlikely that it will be easy, or even possible to say with any degree of certainty, when profitability will arrive for the company.

Because of this, I consider the company's valuation multiples wishful thinking, and I cannot find a good reason why the company would double, let alone quadruple, results by 2028E. The company lacks the conviction, based on historical forecast accuracy, to deliver positive results, in my view. It's one of the very few businesses with a 100% negative miss ratio for forecasts, both on a 1-year and 2-year basis.

I would therefore argue that unless you get the company at a significant discount, I consider it to be worth only the most speculative of investors' time - and even in that scenario, I believe there are more attractive speculative investments out there. In my last article, I gave the company a speculative PT of $8/share. While I retain this target here, and while the company is "closing in" on it, with recent share prices close to $8.9/share, I would argue that even below $8/share, you need to understand that what you're buying is a company with a negative NAV. This puts the company as one of the riskier businesses I cover - albeit with a potentially attractive asset portfolio.

To be clear, I don't consider the asset portfolio the problem as much as the debt used to finance and get the company to where it is today.

Risks and considerations at $9/share, which is where we are today, are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Things like consumer pricing levels, sentiment, and potential company appeal are central to the company's potential here. The fact that revenue is going up, while traffic is going down, and even corporate offline events are going down, shows me a worrying trend that seems to pervade the company's business results. It tells me that the company is extracting more money per customer but from a smaller total number of customers - and I see this not changing in 2Q26, which gives me little hope for 3Q and 4Q of this year.

My updated thesis for April 2026 is as follows.

Thesis

Lucky Strike Entertainment is a potentially interesting company, even apart from owning the company's landlord, VICI Properties (I owned these shares previously, but no longer do). I do believe that LUCK could be fundamentally attractive in 2026, but more likely in 2027 or 2028, when we get more visibility in the company's appeal. I also believe this company comes with significantly more risk than I estimated over a year ago. Forecast accuracy and age remain the problem here. I say the problem is now worse than it was before.

LUCK continues to show a very low historical and forecast-based chance of successfully hitting targets and is currently an EPS-negative business. It should be said that OCF and EBITDA are better ways to value and look at the company. When basing forecasts on this, then the upside is better. I'm still estimating the company to trade at around 7-8x OCF, and I'm forecasting 2027E as the point where the company becomes positive in terms of bottom-line results. This would allow upside. Remember, though, this company is a speculative play. In this market, I would be very careful with speculative plays (it's too volatile - in my view).

I may end up adding the company at a lower price because I like the business at my targeted price, and I like management (and still do here for 2026). However, this is not one for the faint-hearted, so be careful as to how much you buy and if you buy.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.

  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.

  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.

  • This company is currently cheap.

  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $8/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

data.title: Q1 2026 Municipal Credit - A Turn In Credit Quality? War, Federal Funding And Risky Assets, And An Update On NYC Congestion Pricing
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

Municipal credit quality continues to be resilient. S&P reports that upgrades exceeded downgrades in most sectors in 2025, elevating average municipal ratings. Smith’s Research, which combines three rating agency actions, shows similar positive-weighted activity. However, the first quarter of 2026 saw a reversal, with downgrades exceeding upgrades by S&P. It makes sense that the ratio of upgrades to downgrades is narrowing or reversing after several years, with positive ratings actions exceeding negative actions. We have wondered for years when the credit outlook might change, since it has been strong for so long, even with challenges.

Munis have an average rating of AA, reflecting strong economies, improved budgeting tools, and financial cushions to help manage challenges that may lie ahead. Challenges include the ending of pandemic stimulus (some aid still flowed in 2026) that helped many municipalities over the hump of Covid-19 restrictions, changes to federal funding such as FEMA and Medicaid that need to be managed, as well as effects of the Iran war such as increasing oil prices and inflation and cyber risks.

Munis should prevail and usually do. The default rate is practically nonexistent for safe-sector, highly rated bonds. The default rate is extremely low and concentrated on non-rated or lower-rated bonds. The average five-year cumulative default rate from 2015 through 2024 for investment-grade munis was a very low 0.04%, according to Moody’s, which is multiples lower than the 0.85% for investment-grade corporates. States have oversight and monitor local governments, which can act as an early-warning system to identify problems and trigger actions to provide aid in management or finances. Fortunately, states have many revenue sources, the ability to adjust expenses and revenue, and healthy reserve positions.

Effect of War

The Iran War is affecting oil prices and inflation expectations, supply chains – which we have some experience with since the pandemic – and increasing fear of and potential for cyberattacks, including on water and electric infrastructure, much of which is managed by municipalities. All these raise costs for munis and their residents and challenge their management. Oil price increases and volatility ripple through the economy, as the cost and availability of many items rise and there is reduced disposable income to purchase other items, bringing increased concerns of affordability. Supply chains can alter and reduce the flow of goods, affecting ports and other modes of transportation as well as raising prices. Analysts and rating agencies already evaluate cyber risk and the sophistication of munis in managing it. Reductions in the Department of Homeland Security may reduce technical assistance the federal government can provide to munis. Increased cyber risk from war or other sources of hacking raises the cost of combating attacks as well as the cost of cyber insurance. On an ironically bright side, higher prices yield higher sales tax revenue to many munis.

FEMA funding changes would affect some states more than others

Last year, Municipal Market Analytics (MMA) conducted a study comparing disaster aid received to state GDP. In 35 states, including CA and NY, the aggregate amount of FEMA aid obligated from 2015–25 amounted to less than 0.25% of the respective state’s 2024 GDP. In 43 states, had affected munis raised those funds in the bond market, aggregate new-money issuance in that period would have had to increase by less than 5%. This implies that a world without FEMA-style federal aid would have posed only modest long-term risk to state and local ratings, credit spreads, and investor performance.

For the states that are more exposed to disasters – Louisiana, Hawaii, and Florida, and to a lesser extent North Carolina, West Virginia, and Vermont – aggregate FEMA aid ranged from 4% of state GDP to half a percent. If debt had been issued to replace FEMA aid, MMA analysis suggests that bonded debt would be much higher and ratings could be a notch or two lower.

We have seen extreme weather events hit states that were not typically prone to them. These states are less prepared for disasters and may need more help to manage through.

The future of FEMA aid is still being discussed, and the outcome could be less drastic than full elimination. It does appear there will be more direct state involvement and that the focus will be on resiliency and away from rebuilding the same failed infrastructure in the same locations.

Disaster Recovery, Rebuilding, and Resilience Conference

I am a member of the board of the Southern Municipal Finance Authority (SMFS), a local society of the National Federation of Municipal Analysts (NFMA.org). We will be hosting a conference in Asheville NC, which suffered extreme flooding in 2024 in the wake of Hurricane Helene.

We’re bringing together state and local governments, industry experts, finance professionals, and community partners to explore how municipalities can rebuild stronger, smarter, and more equitably in advance of and after natural disasters: Disaster Recovery, Rebuilding, and Resilience Conference. It’s a beautiful time of the year in Asheville, we invite you to look into joining us.

Federal changes and anti-fraud measures

Most agree that greater oversight of programs to combat fraud and improper grant payments should improve governance and expense controls as corrective actions are implemented. However, task force actions may cause delays or reductions in funding or potential penalties, yet another credit challenge for state and local government budgets over the next year or so.

Extreme weather

The funding for disasters aside, the frequency and intensity of weather events from droughts and wildfires to hurricanes and extreme flooding can make a state less desirable to live in. Changing weather has produced wildfires, drought conditions, and extreme rainfall leading to water supply problems that can constrain growth. The fear of damage and disruption, higher insurance costs, and higher utility rates are considerations. Further exacerbating water and electricity issues are data centers, which have extremely large utility needs for processing and cooling. Data centers sometimes receive tax incentives to locate to an area, helping the muni local government but not necessarily the utility ratepayers.

Demographics, population movement, and growth

Warmer-weather states and lower tax rates have long been a draw for retirees; but as noted above, extreme weather and increased costs may be changing that dynamic. The aging of the population and lower birth rates affect the credit quality of higher education and K-12 institutions as enrollment in many areas declines. The growth of charter schools has also challenged the education sector. We have seen the movement from high-tax to low-tax states by individuals as well as businesses; and many lower-cost, middle-of-the-country areas are making their communities more livable and retaining younger folks and retirees who may have thought about moving. How these longer-term trends evolve and how munis manage these changes are slower-moving determinants of credit quality and bear watching.

State tax increases and decreases

States compete in many areas, and tax rates are part of the competition. Wealthy, high-tax jurisdictions are doubling down and looking to increase income taxes. They include NYC and CA, to name a few. Washington State, which does not have an income tax, is instituting a 9.9% tax on incomes over $1 million. This tax is not effective until 2029, so residents have time to prepare or to move. This contrasts with states reducing tax rates to become more competitive, such as Mississippi and Alabama. Then there are states looking to reduce or eliminate property taxes, such as Florida. Property taxes are viewed as a very strong security for the backing of general obligation bonds, and they will remain a source of municipal funding. However, the growth in property taxes in some areas is concerning to residents, and the emphasis on limiting revenue sources will focus management on expenses, efficiencies, and rationalization of services.

NYC rating outlooks revised to negative

In mid-March, Moody’s, Fitch, and Kroll rating agencies changed the outlook for NYC AA-rated GO bonds to negative from stable, based on the mayor’s preliminary budget, which reflects larger than previously reported and widening out-year budget gaps, even with the use of some reserves. Uncertain gap-closing solutions that could require city or state approvals, such as income and property tax increases, are included in the budget or budget discussions. On the positive side, all the rating agencies acknowledge New York City's still-favorable economic conditions.

S&P notes the preliminary budget is unbalanced and includes uncertain revenue assumptions; however, it maintained a stable outlook on the city’s bonds. The stable outlook focuses on the framework and the well-established multifaceted budget development process that generally works to arrive at a balanced budget and a surplus at the end of the fiscal year. S&P expects proposals and negotiations will likely evolve over the coming months – as will the outlook on the city’s bonds.

In November we noted the strong budget framework of New York City that was instituted after the fiscal crisis of 1975 and challenges that the city may face. “NYC Mayoral Election 2025.”

Speaking of NYC – Congestion pricing and MTA

MTA has many funding sources, including proceeds from various state- and city-related bond issues, grants, and subsidies. MTA issues transportation revenue bonds backed by gross revenue, including excess bridge and tunnel tolls. The bonds are rated in the A category. The scale of the system and debt issued makes new revenue sources, like congestion pricing, important for supporting MTA’s capital program and long-term credit quality. The fee charged for traffic traveling below 60th Street during most hours of the day is very high and contested. London was the first large city to institute congestion pricing, and it is successful or at least tolerated there. Much of MTA’s funding comes from tolls collected on the bridges and tunnels entering NYC, whose traffic the congestion pricing has reduced. However, the mass transit system of subways, buses, and trains is the spine of the economic engine of New York City and surrounding areas. The city’s importance to the state is shown by recent state funding increases.

Although controversial, New York’s congestion pricing program has enjoyed strong initial success, generating about $548 million in its first year – exceeding expectations – while reducing vehicle entries into Manhattan’s congestion zone by roughly 12% and contributing to increased transit ridership. This adds diversity of revenue and could add to credit quality. The mechanism, however, needs to survive legal, political, and economic risks. The charge is unpopular and may reduce revenue from bridge traffic.

Pensions

Improving pension funding has been the focus of municipalities, their pensioners, taxpayers, and muni analysts for years now, especially after recognition that underfunding a plan can be more expensive than making regular periodic payments so that the funded level grows and does not decline. The implementation in 2012 of the Government Accounting Standards Board (GASB) pension reporting rules, in part to better inform constituents after the 2007–08 financial crisis, required improved reporting of pension funded status and assumptions used. In times of economic stress, however, not fully funding annual payments to a pension fund may be a tempting way to reduce expenses; and as we enter more uncertain times, pension funding will continue to be evaluated. The trend of fewer workers supporting an expanding number of retirees adds to the importance of discipline in pension funding.

State and large-city pension funding levels have improved. According to an S&P January 2026 report, US public pension funded ratios reached an average of 80% as of the close of fiscal 2025. Pew Charitable Trusts also notes improvements in funded levels.

Private assets, which are generally less transparent and less liquid than publicly traded assets, have increased as pension investments. S&P, using data from Cliffwater.com estimates that private debt and private equity have increased to 19% of state plans while data from the Public Plans database shows increases to private equity for local plans, too. Long-term liabilities like pensions do generally invest in longer-term, higher-yielding investments to combat inflation and to meet future pension payments; however, careful selection is important. Calls for more transparency of private assets are being heard.

State rating changes

Mississippi’s S&P AA rating outlook was changed to stable from negative in mid-March, while Moody’s rates the debt Aa2. The outlook improvement is due to the state’s ongoing commitment to structural budget balance and maintenance of healthy reserves, despite modest revenue declines from recent tax reductions and generally slower economic growth compared with that of the US as a whole. It also reflects pension plan changes that should help improve funding progress in the medium to long term.

On 1/9/2026 New Mexico was upgraded by Moody’s to Aa1 from Aa2, while S&P rates it AA stable. The action is driven by the state's well-established and prudent governance practices that have partially mitigated its reliance on volatile severance taxes. Investment earnings from more substantial permanent fund balances are now a more significant revenue source. There is some negative credit exposure from federal policy priorities that have led to federal job losses and the loss of Medicaid eligibility for approximately 100,000 residents beginning in January 2027. Overall employment growth continues, driven by job gains in other sectors, including state and local government and education.

Conclusion

There are over 50,000 municipalities in the United States, and each of them can issue many different types of debt. General obligation bonds fund school districts and local government improvements to buildings and roads. Munis can issue sales tax-backed bonds for transportation and downtown development, or utility rates and fee-backed bonds for water, sewer, and electric projects. Then there are the not-for-profit entities that issue muni bonds, such as in the healthcare and higher education sectors. And the list goes on. The muni market is about $4 trillion and is overseen by municipal management, elected officials, residents, federal and state governments, rating agencies, and investors. It is an important aspect of funding the infrastructure of this country, in contrast to practices in more centrally planned or bank-financed countries. Legal structures and security provisions in bond documents provide protection to bondholders and allow munis to continue to access the capital markets at lower-cost tax-exempt rates to fund services. This is important, as muni bond issuance is expected to increase again this year to $600 billion from $580 billion in 2025 and from an average of $400-$450 billion over the last decade.

There are many challenges ahead for munis. Some are long-tailed, and some are more immediate. If the economy turns to recession, which is not anticipated at this time, these challenges will be accentuated, and we would expect more downgrades, depending on methods management uses to address the challenges. Strong reserve and rainy-day fund balances, improved budget management, well-funded pensions, and a generally growing economy all bode well for the future of muni credit quality.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Cybin: Regulatory Tailwinds Unlock Asymmetric Upside (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

I'm choosing to now upgrade my rating on Cybin Inc. (HELP) since we’ve seen quite a few recent regulatory and clinical shifts in favor of the company’s trajectory. The biggest beneficiary I see from the recent policy shift is likely HLP003. It’s their lead psilocybin-based candidate for major depressive disorder, and given it’s already in an advanced stage with a strong early efficacy profile, I think it’s now in a decent position to take advantage of accelerated FDA pathways. We should also see some increased institutional support tied to PTSD and broader mental health indications. Now, at the same time, the valuation has become unusually attractive.

The market is still pricing the business close to cash value here and assigning just limited value to their pipeline, even with the clear catalysts ahead, as I’ll explain. For investors, the big milestones to watch would include further Phase 2/3 data readouts for HLP003 and progress on HLP004 for anxiety disorders. We could also see updates from management tied to regulatory engagement under the new framework in the next earnings call. So taken together, I see a favorable risk-reward skew emerging here and one that supports my upgrade to a Buy rating with the new policy tailwinds. For a review of HLP003, I went over the progress so far on the candidate in detail in my previous coverage.

Trump Orders Faster PTSD Research

As you know, Cybin has seen a recent surge, as have quite a few psychedelic-related stocks. Specifically, this would be a nice tailwind from a policy shift signaled by President Trump’s executive order. Essentially, the aim of the Trump administration here is to accelerate research, regulatory review, and eventually patients’ access to certain therapies involving substances like psilocybin, MDMA, LSD, and ibogaine.

Now, accelerating access to drugs such as LSD does sound a bit dramatic, and we should keep in mind that historically, these kinds of compounds have been classified under Schedule I of the U.S. Controlled Substances Act. This basically means they’re deemed to have no accepted medical use and a high potential for abuse. On the surface, this looks true, and that classification has been one of the biggest bottlenecks for companies like Cybin that operate in this space. You see, since that list of drugs is essentially restricted from use, Cybin and others faced a lot of restrictions if they wanted to conduct any kind of clinical research on them.

As for the new order, this, of course, doesn’t legalize these kinds of drugs outright, and it actually doesn’t even reschedule them, but what it does do is meaningfully lower a lot of friction in the system. What it basically does is direct agencies like the FDA and HHS to prioritize psychedelic research and to also introduce some expedited review pathways. The latter here would include some priority vouchers that could potentially shrink approval timelines from years to months. There should also be federal support for studies as a result, and that would be particularly true for conditions like PTSD and traumatic brain injury in veterans. I think of it as creating a fast lane for certain companies that are already deep into clinical development. So we could be looking at some potential to compress timelines to commercialization and somewhat derisk regulatory uncertainty. This is one reason why the market reacted so aggressively.

Implications For Cybin

As for Cybin specifically, I see this order having a few implications since their pipeline is already aligned with the exact conditions policymakers are prioritizing. For example, we can take Cybin’s lead candidate, HLP003, which is a deuterated psilocybin analogue currently in advanced clinical trials for major depressive disorder. We’ve seen some early data that has suggested faster onset, shorter duration, and potentially a much more scalable treatment protocol compared to the traditional psilocybin therapies on the market.

Now, all of these are pretty critical if we want to see real-world adoption. And under the new policy framework, I can see HLP003 potentially benefiting from accelerated FDA interactions/priority review pathways. Overall, it’s basically like an increased institutional willingness to fund or even partner on late-stage trials. So that should directly lower development risk and shorten time-to-market. Keep in mind that time to market is the single biggest value driver for small, pre-revenue biotech firms.

Valuation

You may have also seen that Cybin was tipped as one of the most undervalued stocks with active psychedelic research relative to the peer group here. I think that’s mainly because, despite having a comparatively pretty advanced and differentiated pipeline, their valuation has lagged a bit. As per Seeking Alpha, they have a higher value grade versus competitors like COMPASS Pathways or even Atai Life Sciences, which are trading at far richer multiples. In other words, I don’t think the market has fully priced in Cybin’s probability of success here or the impact of regulatory acceleration. So for investors, this creates a bit of asymmetry in that if the executive order successfully translates into faster approvals between about 2027 and 2030, Cybin could stand to benefit disproportionately. The reasoning here is that they can move from clinical-stage uncertainty to revenue generation much faster than previously expected while still being priced more conservatively today.

As for the valuation profile, based on appropriate metrics, Cybin still screens as unusually inexpensive for a clinical-stage biotech. Based on balance sheet and asset-based measures, the company trades at only 0.84x forward price-to-book, a pretty steep discount to the broader healthcare sector, which is at about 3.2x, and even to their own historical average. Their enterprise value sits at roughly $85 million and is also quite modest relative to the $195 million cash sum held. So this tells me the market is assigning only limited value to the pipeline here, beyond that position. Also, keep in mind that Cybin is making late-stage clinical progress. For investors, again, it’s a bit of a disconnect, with a low EV relative to cash and discounted book value. I think this helps explain why Cybin carries that top-tier valuation grade. The market pricing has yet to fully account for their development assets or the potential acceleration from those recent regulatory tailwinds.

Looking Ahead, The Upcoming Pathway

Looking ahead, the focus for me is still on HLP003, but my outlook is now a lot more positive. It’s their deuterated psilocybin analogue, which is currently being developed as an adjunctive treatment for major depressive disorder. Right now, it’s entering Phase 3 pivotal trials as part of the PARADIGM program, with dosing already underway. I think it stands to benefit from the policy shift simply because it is already late-stage and targeting core mental health indications like depression/PTSD-adjacent. Now, the standard pathway is a fairly typical biotech progression that hasn’t changed. They still need to complete Phase 3 pivotal trials with statistically robust efficacy and safety data. After that, there will likely be an NDA to the FDA if the data is positive. So under normal conditions, that review process alone can take about 10 to 12 months, or even longer with delays, followed by scheduling and labeling negotiations. So even a successful program could potentially take years to move from late-stage trials to actual commercialization here.

What changes specifically with the policy shift is not the scientific bar itself, but rather the speed and friction of the system. In terms of catalysts, the key near-term milestone has to be Phase 3 top-line data from the APPROACH study. We can expect this sometime in 4Q26, alongside additional readouts from the broader Phase 3 program.

data.title: Imperial Petroleum: Why The Buyback Is A Distraction - Maintaining Strong Sell
data.description:
Imperial Petroleum Inc. (IMPP) is a company that owns and operates a small fleet of tankers and dry bulk ships. I have covered the company previously, rating it a "Strong Sell" due to poor corporate governance.

I am covering it again due to the buyback announcement, which has caused a rally in the share price since my coverage.

One Small Buyback Vs. One Big Offering

The heart of the issue seems to be the $10 million buyback authorization. However, to fully understand why I view this buyback as a distraction, we must put the buyback into context.

To understand the company's history of capital allocation, I'd like to draw your eyes towards the recent $60 million offering done on Dec 1st, 2025. Specifically, on the fact that the offering diluted the common share count from 36.22 million to 45.75 million. In other words, the company has diluted its shareholders by 20.8% through this offering.

The company has repurchased 251 thousand shares to date with the buyback program, according to the latest earnings release. This is approximately 0.55% of the float, which is clearly much less than the 20.8% dilution that was done just before that.

If the rest of the buyback program were to be executed at the current share price of $4.35, the remaining buyback authorization would retire approximately 2.1 million shares. This represents a new common share count of 43.40 million if the buyback were to be fully executed. Note that this is still higher than the original 36.22 million by nearly 16.5%.

The offering also produced double the amount of shares issued in warrants exercisable at $6.30, namely the Class F and Class G warrants. This forms an effective "wall" for investors in the form of 19 million shares worth of dilution at any price above $6.30.

Adjusted Share Count

Upon looking at the report, a figure caught my attention. That would be the reported 38 million weighted average share count. This is starkly different from the share count reported in the recent offering. Why is that?

The reason is the time period of reporting. In accounting, you count your shares based on a weighted average of how many shares were outstanding throughout the period.

Most companies typically also report shares outstanding on the date of the period ending. This company can do things differently due to being a foreign issuer, which grants it lower regulatory requirements when it comes to financial reporting, as stated in their 20-F filing ("As a foreign private issuer, we are entitled to claim an exemption from certain Nasdaq corporate governance standards..."). This leads to discrepancies such as this one.

Since the company neglected to showcase its shares outstanding count, I'll help them by doing it myself and then calculating earnings per share based on that.

As we know, the current true share count is 45.5 million if we grab the share count post-offering and subtract it by the buybacks done to date. To get a true "diluted" share count, I'll also add 3 items that I saw as significant.

  1. 3.16 million shares issuable under Class E warrants.
  2. 1.65 million shares issuable under an acquisition of 3 bulkers.
  3. 0.7 million shares issuable under stock options.

Adding these 3 items to the current true share count, we get a diluted share count of 51.01 million. When dividing this number by the net income, we get an EPS of $0.29. This is approximately 25% lower than the EPS quoted by management, which sits at $0.39.

By all metrics, this figure is disappointing, especially considering the significant strength of the tanker market in Q4.

Discount To NAV?

As previously mentioned in my original article, there remain various reasons to be skeptical of any meaningful share rerating towards management's self-declared NAV figure of $508.03 million.

Firstly, let us calculate what this self-declared NAV figure translates into on a per-share basis. I will calculate the basic NAV per share using the 51.01 million figure. When dividing the self-declared NAV by the adjusted share outstanding figure, we get an estimated NAV per share of $9.96.

Keen readers will note that this is above the exercise price of the Class F and Class G warrants ($6.30), which are currently numbered at 19 million warrants outstanding when combined. This creates a "diluted" share count of 70.1 million, which brings the fully diluted NAV per share estimate to $7.26 per share.

Now, when comparing this to the share price at the time of writing ($4.35), this is a discount to NAV of 40.1%. I acknowledge that this is a large discount and that this is the main argument the bulls make. My next section will dismantle any argument that claims that such a NAV discount can be unlocked.

Why The Discount Is Justified

First of all, we must acknowledge that the company's management is effectively tied to the company. While not necessarily a bad thing, this means that extra due diligence and scrutiny of management are needed to ensure they are acting in the interests of the common shareholder.

Why is management tied to the company? Mr. Harry Vafias effectively controls a disproportionately high voting power due to his ownership of 16,000 Series B Preferred Shares, which grant him 25,000 votes per share. This grants him 49.99% voting power (per the company's bylaws, which forbid anyone from holding an outright majority), which is more than enough to make decisions effectively on his own.

With that being said, what does the CEO do with this effective control over the company? He uses the company's cash, which is typically acquired by diluting common shareholders at relatively low share prices (as showcased by the recent below-NAV offering), to acquire ships from his privately owned entity, Brave Maritime.

If that doesn't ring alarm bells, take a look at (CISS). It is a spin-off, managed by Harry Vafias in a similar manner, which has led to massive losses for shareholders to date.

The Verdict

All in all, the buyback does not change my mind on the company one bit. I view the governance discount as justified. Not only that, but it can get far wider.

The main risk to my thesis is the fact that this is mainly a trading vehicle and that management may do something unpredictable that may harm my bearish thesis. I cannot control or predict short-term price movements, and neither can most people. The same goes for the management.

I therefore maintain my counter-consensus rating of "Strong Sell" and consider the company uninvestable until there is a shift in management, which I doubt will happen any time soon.

data.title: Travere Therapeutics: Still Undervalued Post FSGS Approval
data.description:
Travere Therapeutics overview

In my previous article titled "Travere Therapeutics: Filspari's FSGS Approval Chance Is Higher Than You Think", I explained why I believe Filspari will be approved for FSGS. Recently that approval came, and TVTX stock shot up on the news. In this article, I will dive into the details of that approval and the commercial prospects. Overall, I think TVTX is still undervalued based on projected revenue and therefore is a Buy at these levels.

Filspari's approval for FSGS

On April 13, 2026, the FDA granted full approval to Filspari (Sparsentan) for the treatment of focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), making it the first and only medication specifically approved for this rare kidney disease. Indicated for both adults and pediatric patients aged 8 years and older who do not have nephrotic syndrome. The drug works by targeting dual pathways to reduce proteinuria and slow kidney damage. This landmark decision was supported by the Phase 3 DUPLEX study, which demonstrated a significant 46% reduction in proteinuria over 108 weeks compared to standard-of-care irbesartan. Despite an earlier regulatory delay and a primary endpoint miss regarding long-term kidney function in the overall study population, the FDA's approval underscores the clinical importance of proteinuria reduction in managing FSGS and provides a critical new option for a patient population that previously relied on off-label therapies.

The key thing to focus here is although this is a full approval, it does come with a restrictor which says the drug can only be used for patients who do not have nephrotic syndrome (NS). This is based on the phase 3 DUPLEX study sub-population analysis, where it showed patients without NS benefited more consistently from Filspari treatment.

Nephrotic syndrome exclusion: Not a huge concern

Nephrotic syndrome is, and this is from the CMO of TVTX:"Nephrotic syndrome is a clinical diagnosis, which commonly refers to meeting all 3 of the following criteria: high levels of proteinuria greater than 3.5 grams per day, low serum albumin of less than 3 grams per deciliter and the presence of edema."

This is not a huge concern, as the CMO of TVTX estimated only 20% of the FSGS patients enrolled in DUPLEX had active NS, and they come in and out of it, and once they are no longer in NS, they can be treated with Filspari. In other words, once the FSGS patient no longer meets any of the 3 NS criteria, they become eligible for Filspari treatments. So the actual % of FSGS patients who are not eligible for Filspari treatments is likely even less than 20% of the total 30,000 patient population.

Filspari in FSGS: Constructive on the launch

I am bullish on the commercial launch of Filspari in FSGS for two key reasons. First, Filspari is the only FDA-approved drug for this indication.
