Second, there is significant "cross-selling" potential with the IgAN indication. TVTX’s successful IgAN launch allows them to leverage existing commercial infrastructure—including prescribers, payers, and CMC production—for FSGS. This represents a unique cross-selling opportunity because the two indications share so many similarities, which I believe should reduce commercialization costs while increasing market penetration.

There's also a significant unmet need in FSGS treatment, as roughly 30% to 50% of patients with primary FSGS potentially progress to end-stage renal disease (ESRD) and need a kidney transplant, and even then up to 30-40% relapse after a kidney transplant (see below figure).

TVTX stock valuation with FSGS approval

The CEO of TVTX mentioned 3B peak sales now with FSGS full approval:

"As we look out, we see the potential for peak year sales over $3 billion when we look at the combination of these 2 indications. And our job right now, as Peter mentioned, is to make sure that we're out there very quickly because patients deserve it and to be able to educate physicians on this label and the opportunity to do better in FSGS."

That is not a far stretch, but even if we use a more bearish projection of $2B peak sales for both indications combined and a PS ratio of 3, we still reach a valuation of $6B. As of today, TVTX is valued at $3.72B, indicating that the company's market cap should rise over time if they execute well.

Another point to note is that TVTX can be very profitable in just a year or so. According to Seeking Alpha data, the consensus EPS could be nearly $4.6 as early as next year, with a forward PE of 8.84. Considering the sector-wide median forward PE of ~18, and again this indicates that TVTX is still significantly undervalued.

From a market penetration perspective, looking at Filspari in IgAN in 2025, total revenue in 2025 was $322M, the per-patient per year listing price was $120k, and Gross-to-Net (GTN) reduction was about 20%. As such, the total number of IgAN patients treated in 2025 was about:

$322M/($120k X 80%) = $3,354

Since there are a total of 70,000 IgAN patients treatable with Filspari, this indicates only ~5% market penetration as of 2025 (in line with management's estimate of current penetration being <10%). If we assume peak market penetration of 20%, and GTN deteriorates to a 28% reduction due to expanding access, peak sales for Filspari in IgAN alone could be:

70,000 X 20% X $120k X 72% = $1.2B

For FSGS revenue, because per patient the dose of Filspari is 800-mg which doubles that of IgAN, per patient revenue is much higher, say 50% more at $180k per patient per year. Since there is much less competition in FSGS than IgAN, we will assume peak market penetration of 25% (lower due to the NS exclusion requirement), with GTN reduction of 25%. This gives a peak revenue of:

30,000 X 25% X $180k X 75% = 1B

As such, we reach a total peak revenue of $2.2B, not so far from the CEO's $3B prediction. This goes to show that if the commercial execution goes well, there is significant upside potential for TVTX.

Risks

The main risks are that the commercial launch of Filspari in FSGS and the continued expansion in IgAN fail to impress. IgAN is becoming a crowded field with major players like Novartis and Vertex entering the market. However, FSGS is currently a significant "white space," with Filspari serving as the only FDA-approved drug. I imagine that in the coming quarters, investors will weigh FSGS PSF numbers and pricing heavily to assess the overall commercial launch in this key indication. Given the synergies with the existing IgAN commercial infrastructure, I believe the launch will be both rapid and expansive.

The other risk is patent expiration or LOE. Filspari (sparsentan) holds key patent protections and FDA exclusivities, including a major patent (US9993461) expiring March 29, 2030, and an IgAN indication exclusivity running through February 17, 2030. TVYX management expects Filspari to have LOE until at least 2033 due to expected orphan drug exclusivity and is working to expand it further. 7 years is still a long time to make the best of it, and I would argue this potentially makes TVTX a good buyout target given the recent increased interest in kidney diseases by big pharma.

Final remarks

Post-FSGS approval, TVTX looks undervalued based on both peak sales and forward PE. If the company is able to launch Filspari in FSGS successfully in the upcoming quarters, a significant re-rating of its stock price is likely. Using a peak sale of $2.2B and PS of 3, TVTX's fair valuation should be between $5-$6.6B today, and that is not counting any pipeline progress. Overall, I rate TVTX a BUY.

data.title: AMD Stock: My $300 Target Is Close - Here's What Comes Next
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares have been getting a major boost in recent weeks as AI spending takes center stage, reported shortages are increasing CPU prices, and the company's data center business continues to boom. I remain bullish on AMD's prospects going into the next earnings report on May 5th and into the second half of 2026.

In my last piece on AMD back in February, titled "$300 Appears Imminent; Why I'm Buying More," I highlighted the company's strong operating results in the data center and argued valuation multiple convergence would lift the stock to all-time highs in short order. Less than two months since that call, shares are already up 40% and are closing in on my target. That article can be read here.

So what caused the sudden momentum shift? There are probably a few factors at play: the easing of tensions in the Iran war led equities to all-time highs, CPUs appear to be facing a supply shortage, and AI infrastructure stocks have been surging in general as massive capex comes to fruition. Whatever the exact catalysts, I think this has been a long time coming— AMD has been executing on its roadmap and delivering on its promises to narrow the gap with the top of the compute food chain. It was only a matter of time, in my opinion, before the stock price began to reflect that reality.

In recent weeks, AMD has undeniably been getting a boost, along with Intel (INTC), from reports that the AI boom is causing a shortage in processors, which could lead to a 15% increase in price for general purpose server CPUs in 2026. If this sounds familiar, that might be because the exact same thing is playing out in the memory market as prices are soaring in what many are dubbing the "RAMpocalypse." In response, the stock of DRAM and NAND manufacturer Micron (MU) is up 570% over the last 12 months. Is this a glimpse into AMD's future?

On the one hand, a Digitimes report recently claimed that the CPU shortage is "more acute" than the memory shortage because memory is still available in limited quantities but CPUs are sold out for months. On the other hand, while you might initially think this implies that AMD and Intel are about to see a Micron-like boom, there is one, vital reason why this is highly unlikely to be the case: volume.

CPU volumes are substantially lower than DRAM and NAND by orders of magnitude, meaning a shortage in the former translates into higher margins and modestly higher revenue while a shortage in the latter translates into earning multiple years worth of net income in a single quarter. So the shortage you've been hearing about in the news likely doesn't mean that AMD and INTC are going to double in the next month, but it does mean that CPUs are supply constrained and in high demand, which will keep prices and margins high.

And, similar to what we've seen in the memory market, the shortage is concentrated in the high-end data center market, but since production lines are limited, processor manufacturers will shift away from low-end markets towards higher margin customers and the price increases and shortages will ripple down the supply chain. For AMD, this could have the effect of boosting margins throughout all of its CPU segments, which is exactly what happened to Micron:

Even low-margin divisions like Automotive & Embedded and Mobile & Client saw margins shoot higher and, if a CPU supply shortage persists, I think we might see a similar effect on AMD's lower margin segments like Embedded and Gaming and also, of course, on its higher margin Client and Data Center segments.

Part of this shortage can be attributed to Intel's slower-than-expected rollout of its next-generation 18A process node, which the company has promised will challenge TSMC's (TSM) dominance in premium silicon. I have long been bullish about this node, and though yields have not yet improved enough for true high-volume manufacturing, Intel has been able to produce its own processors using the technology even if yields are not yet high enough to procure and manufacture chips for external customers.

The current scarcity environment has proven favorable to AMD, which contracts its processors through TSMC — a company that is most assuredly not having yield issues. Intel is sending out Clearwater Forest server CPUs built on 18A to a few data center customers and expects a ramp in 2H2026, but delays have plagued the company's newest process nodes for over a decade so whether that comes to pass or whether the market remains in a deep supply shortage remains to be seen. Personally, I'm betting we end up getting both of those scenarios.

This all also happens to be well timed with the launch of AMD's latest line of server CPUs, codenamed Venice, which is slated for a 2H2026 release date. Venice features the Zen 6 architecture and will be produced on TSMC's bleeding-edge N2P node, which claims a nearly 20% performance boost with a 36% reduction in power over the N3E process used to manufacture Turin Dense. With TSMC at its back, AMD has stormed back in the market share race with Intel (data is from Mercury Research):

Previously languishing in the single-digits a decade ago, AMD now commands a unit share and revenue share of 36.4% and 42.6%, respectively, in the desktop CPU market and 28.8% and 41.3% unit and revenue share in server CPUs. And the trend has been relentless.

While unit share is, of course, important, revenue share is the true measure of sales that translate to net income, and on that front, AMD is up 1460 bps YoY in desktop and 490 bps in server as a process node advantage, courtesy of TSMC, has given EPYC chips a leg-up in efficiency and power over Intel's offerings. This gap is especially pronounced in the data center where performance per watt is essential and hyperscalers can ill afford to cheap out. This means that, until 18A achieves the requisite yields to actually make a dent in industry demand, AMD will remain far-and-away the leader in premium CPUs.

And beyond the current favorable pricing environment and AMD's CPU leadership, the company has also continued to push the envelope in the AI accelerator market against the likes of Nvidia (NVDA). While Nvidia is still the king of training, AMD's MI455X, expected to release in the second half of 2026, is an inference powerhouse that delivers raw performance on par with Nvidia's Blackwell and boasts a massive 432 GB of HBM4 memory, which dwarfs Blackwell's 192 GB / 288 GB (B200 vs. B300) and significantly outpaces next-generation Vera Rubin's 288 GB. There are certainly other metrics that matter in this fight where Nvidia still has an edge, such as bandwidth and speed, but AMD has undoubtedly closed the gap and has cemented itself as a viable alternative in a sector that needs all the capacity it can get.

Lastly, AMD will also be launching its Helios rack-scale servers in tandem with the MI455X, which will allow it to compete with Nvidia on providing a completely vertically-integrated AI stack to data center operators. These racks are key to AMD's ability to challenge Nvidia for hyperscaler business, which has already been paying dividends.

Investor Takeaway

I think it's fair to say that AMD is firing on all cylinders right now across all of its product segments; with CPUs in short supply, pricing is ticking up, which could lead to record margins in the coming quarters. Shares are already rising quite sharply towards my $300 milestone, but I still think there is more room to run in the short-term on the back of the ongoing supply shortage and the AI sector's current major momentum upswing. Market volatility has remained elevated and I think the sector is still quite vulnerable to large swings so those with a low risk tolerance might want to be cautious.

In the long-term, I continue to see AMD as an undervalued opportunity at the forefront of powerful secular trends. The company is an innovator that constantly matches and leapfrogs well-funded opposition using a strong culture of engineering excellence and strategic thinking. As such, I am reiterating a Strong Buy on AMD and will be adding on pullbacks on the way up.

Thanks for reading!
